Today, the White House released National Security Presidential Memorandum 12 (NSPM-12), establishing a new framework for the cybersecurity of U.S. National Security Systems.  The memorandum re-establishes the Committee on National Security Systems, expands the role of the National Security Agency as the National Manager for National Security Systems, strengthens incident reporting requirements, and directs agencies to adopt more unified cybersecurity standards across military, intelligence, and federal networks.  Whether you work in government, defense, critical infrastructure, energy, telecommunications, or cybersecurity, this document is worth reading.  A few key themes stand out:  • Increased accountability for agencies operating national security systems  • Faster reporting and response to cyber incidents  • Greater emphasis on secure cloud environments and emerging technologies  • Stronger coordination between military, intelligence, and civilian agencies  • Continued focus on defending critical missions against sophisticated nation-state cyber threats  Cybersecurity is no longer just an IT issue. It is a national security issue, an economic security issue, and increasingly a public safety issue.  As threats continue to evolve, the ability to secure systems, protect sensitive information, and maintain operational resilience will remain one of the defining challenges of our time.  Read the memorandum and draw your own conclusions.  #Cybersecurity #NationalSecurity #CriticalInfrastructure #CyberDefense #RiskManagement #InformationSecurity #InfrastructureProtection #Technology #Government #Leadership

Incident Alert: Incident: Terrorist Attack | Govt Response ID: 20260512 | 19:30 UTC Loc: Fino SOG Camp, Mandera County, Kenya  Issues: Suspected Al-Shabaab militants launched an attack on the Fino Special Operations Group (SOG) Camp in Mandera County. The attackers engaged security personnel in a firefight lasting approximately 25 minutes.  Militants used Rocket Propelled Grenades (RPGs) and PKM machine guns during the assault before being repelled by security forces and forced to retreat from the area.  Three officers injured. No fatalities reported. Multiple suspected injuries on the militants side.  Security agencies have since reinforced the camp and surrounding areas, while a pursuit operation has been launched to locate and apprehend the attackers.  Assessment: The attack highlights the persistent threat posed by Al-Shabaab along Kenya's northeastern border regions. The group's continued ability to conduct cross-border raids and attacks against security installations underscores its operational intent despite sustained counterterrorism operations in the region.  Advisory: Personnel operating within Mandera County, particularly in Lafey, Arabia, Elwak, and surrounding border areas, should maintain heightened vigilance. Security forces are likely to increase patrols, checkpoints, and clearance operations in the coming days. Non-essential travel near security installations and remote border areas should be minimized.  ASCI continues to monitor the situation and will provide updates as further information becomes available.

And tell Musk there's no such thing as citizen journliasm without  a press card: that's illegitimate and prone to inaccuracy, conflation and fraud. Viral hysteria is what caused the Pandemic. Out of how many people. Is it an increase or is it the same as last year. Even professional journalists miss this point: Context. Level-headed reporting of facts, not inference, and not the monetised controversy-likes that Musk wants to pay his debts off.

A hazardous materials scare briefly disrupted the Pentagon but officials say testing found no hazard.  Reuters reported that the Pentagon issued a brief shelter-in-place order after an air-quality warning triggered concern about a possible hazardous-materials incident.  Arlington Fire & EMS said its units, including its Hazardous Materials Team, responded in support of the Pentagon Force Protection Agency’s hazmat team.  The key update: this appears to have been a false alarm.  Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell said subsequent testing confirmed no hazard existed and that normal operations resumed.  This is not the story: “hazardous substance confirmed at the Pentagon.”  The story is that a sensor alert or air quality warning was serious enough to trigger emergency protocols, partial lockdown measures, hazmat response, and testing inside one of the most sensitive buildings in the U.S. government.  The questions now are procedural:  • What triggered the alert? • Was it a faulty sensor? • How quickly did building systems isolate the risk? • Were employees clearly instructed? • Did the response work as designed?  False alarm or not, this is a useful reminder: modern security is not just guards and gates. It is sensors, ventilation systems, emergency protocols, and rapid coordination between federal and local responders.  Sources: reuters.com/world/us/fire-of… abcnews.go.com/US/authoritie… axios.com/2026/06/11/pentago… #Pentagon #PublicSafety #Hazmat #NationalSecurity #EmergencyResponse

From The Bureau substack (Sam Cooper)  "TORONTO – At 5:40 in the morning on June 11, heavily armed tactical officers breached a fourth-floor unit at 15 Martha Eaton Way, a high-rise in the city’s northwest that has been marked by gang gunfire for decades. They were executing one of several search warrants tied to a series of shootings across the city — including the March 10 attack on the United States consulate, an incident American prosecutors allege was directed from abroad by a commander of an Iranian proxy militia. Within moments, Constable Marc Pinizzotto, a 43-year-old tactical veteran and father, was shot dead. A 19-year-old resident, Nicholas Bennett, was struck multiple times by return fire and will be charged with first-degree murder. A second 19-year-old, Zara Jabbi, wanted in connection with the consulate shooting, remains at large. The Martha Eaton tower, like the similar tenements of the nearby Jane and Finch corridor, resembles a Chicago or Los Angeles of the north, with numerous “Crip” sets of teenage shooters and street dealers moving cocaine, fentanyl, and methamphetamine for brokers whose supply lines ultimately trace to the networks exposed in Toronto Police’s Project Brisa, which focused on the North American distribution pipeline of Ryan Wedding and his Mexican cartel suppliers. To understand what perished with Constable Pinizzotto, it helps to separate what is known from what is not — because the gap between the two is where Canada’s most dangerous national security problem now lives. Here is what is known. A federal criminal complaint unsealed in Manhattan alleges that the consulate shooting, and an attack on a Toronto synagogue, were claimed by Mohammad Baqer Saad Dawood Al-Saadi, a 32-year-old commander of Kata’ib Hizballah, the Iraqi militia that operates as a proxy of Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. Captured on a wiretap ten days after gunmen fired on the consulate, Al-Saadi boasted that “our people” carried out that attack and the strike on a Toronto synagogue that he strangely called “the Knesset.” The complaint alleges that since the opening of the American-Israeli military campaign against Iran in late February, his network directed or promoted at least 18 attacks across Europe and two in Canada — and that it did so not through sleeper cells of ideological loyalists, but through a procurement system that any logistics manager would recognize. Attacks were financed through currency exchange houses — an economy of Iranian underground banking and sanctions evasion tied to Middle Eastern, Mexican and Chinese criminal networks, which The Bureau has documented well for three years now — with refunds built in if the violence failed to materialize. “If you send the Mafia, they can pick a place of their choosing,” Al-Saadi explained to a source reporting to the Federal Bureau of Investigation. “If they hit, then that’s good, but if they don’t, then I get my money back. This is how the deal is.” He attempted to hire what he believed was a Mexican cartel operative to burn synagogues in New York, Los Angeles, and Arizona. Here is what is not known. Canadian authorities have not stated that the young men targeted in the Martha Eaton Way raids are connected to Al-Saadi’s network. But they, along with the Royal Canadian Mounted Police and likely Canadian intelligence officials, have been working with the FBI — and U.S. Ambassador Pete Hoekstra, a former chair of the House Intelligence Committee, has taken a close personal interest in the case. What The Bureau,Canadian investigators, and at least one other news organization have established instead is a local milieu. The Globe and Mail, citing a source with knowledge of the investigation, reported that the raid connects to a wider probe of a shooters-for-hire network — one allegedly responsible not only for the consulate attack but for gunfire directed at the properties of a waste-management giant, at tow-truck businesses, and at private homes. That milieu is easily recognizable to anyone who follows crime in Toronto, where underage shooters — often including murderers whom police are legally barred from naming — have become the sicarios of the north. Police associations across Canada have repeatedly warned that the country’s youth justice laws are being used as shields by serious organized crime in this shooters-for-rent market. The Martha Eaton building itself sits in territory long contested by Crip-affiliated street gangs, and its recent history is a chronicle of juvenile gun violence: last August, eight-year-old JahVai Roy was killed in his bed by stray rounds fired toward the building’s playground, and three teenagers were charged with first-degree murder. The suspects charged in two of the March synagogue shootings are 18 and 17 years old. There is, as of today, no proven chain of command running from Tehran to the fourth floor of 15 Martha Eaton Way. What there is, on the face of the American complaint and the Canadian charge sheets, is something more disturbing — a marketplace. Teenage shooters who will fire on a rival fentanyl dealer, a synagogue, a consulate, a tow truck, or a child’s bedroom window with equal ease — for whoever is paying — brokered through layers designed so the shooter never knows the client. In an economy like that, the foreign sponsor is the last thing Canadian investigators can prosecute, if they find it at all. And it is not an isolated discovery. Over the past six months, Canadian court filings have surfaced the same architecture again and again, with different clients at the top. Consider Project South, the Toronto police corruption investigation that authorities describe as among the gravest in modern Canadian history. At its center is Brian Da Costa, an alleged Toronto trafficker facing 16 charges, whose network is accused of bribing serving Toronto police officers and conspiring to murder a senior corrections official at the Toronto South Detention Centre — reportedly because that official was too effective at keeping drugs out of the facility. Police initially disclosed only obliquely that it was a fentanyl, cocaine, marijuana and meth distribution network with serious international ties. As The Bureau later reported, court filings now place Gurpreet Singh on Da Costa’s no-contact list. Singh is the alleged Indo-Canadian trucking operative indicted in the United States as a key cross-border smuggler for Ryan Wedding, the former Olympian turned Sinaloa Cartel heavyweight whose organization, American prosecutors allege, moved hundreds of kilograms of cocaine into Canada, ordered the Medellín assassination of a federal witness, paid a Brampton lawyer roughly $1 million to penetrate Canadian police investigations, and bribed Canadian officials. Singh has been held since October 2024 at Toronto South — the same jail Da Costa’s network allegedly sought to supply, and whose drug-blocking official it allegedly sought to kill. A second supervisor at the facility, who according to the Toronto Star oversaw the unit housing Singh and visited his cell with unusual frequency, has had her seized devices searched under warrant in the same probe; she has not been charged. Then consider the case that fuses the two threat streams — cartel and state — into a single Canadian defendant. In 2024, federal prosecutors in Minnesota unsealed an indictment charging Damion Patrick John Ryan, a full-patch Hells Angels member from British Columbia, with conspiring in a murder-for-hire plot against an Iranian defector and his companion living in Maryland. The client was the network of Naji Sharifi Zindashti, a drug lord American and British officials say carries out assassinations and kidnappings of dissidents on behalf of Iranian intelligence. The negotiations ran over an encrypted phone service built by a defunct Vancouver company. A Canadian biker, recruited by Tehran’s outsourced killers, to silence refugees on American soil: the contract model of Al-Saadi’s money-exchange terrorism, three years early, with a Canadian gang as the vendor. And if there were any doubt that this model has matured into a direct threat against Canadian public figures, the United States Department of Justice removed it in March. As The Bureau reported exclusively, American prosecutors announced the court-authorized seizure of four websites operated by Iran’s Ministry of Intelligence and Security, dismantling a regime-run cyber and psychological operations network that issued a $250,000 bounty directing the Jalisco New Generation Cartel — an organization Canada itself designated as a terrorist entity in February 2025 — to behead former Ontario legislator Goldie Ghamari at her Ottawa home. The supporting affidavit, filed by the FBI in Maryland, states that the cartel had already been given the home addresses of its targets when the bounty was issued. An Iranian intelligence ministry, contracting a Mexican cartel, to execute a Canadian politician in Canada’s capital: every layer of the marketplace, assembled in a single American court filing. The annual public report of CSIS, published in March, names the Iranian-linked group behind the operation — the Handala Hack Team — but only in connection with the doxxing of a Canadian journalist, omitting any mention of the cartel bounty on a Canadian politician, even as the same report warns in the abstract that hostile states “sometimes hire organized crime groups or proxies” to project their reach into Canada. The same month, pressed by CTV News on whether foreign-directed activity poses a public safety threat, RCMP Commissioner Mike Duheme answered: “what we have in our holdings is we have people that are intimidating people, harassing people, but connecting the dots to a foreign entity, regardless of the country, we don’t have that.” Lay these cases side by side and the pattern is unmistakable. Mexican cartels and Iranian proxies are not merely drawing on the same Canadian service economy — encrypted communications, trucking and logistics cells, corrupt insiders, compromised professionals, and a deep bench of young shooters in neighborhoods the state has effectively ceded — they are, in the Ghamari case, contracting one another inside it. And the violence is climbing the institutional ladder. A jail official marked for assassination. Police officers on a trafficker’s payroll. Now a tactical officer killed serving a warrant in a terrorism-linked probe, two days after an Ontario Provincial Police constable died on duty. Against this, the response of Prime Minister Mark Carney’s government has been inadequate where it has been visible at all. Ottawa’s signature effort against the antisemitic violence of recent months has amounted to statements of condemnation while three synagogues absorbed gunfire within a single week in March and a proxy commander in Iraq bragged about hitting “the Knesset” in Toronto. There is still no Canadian equivalent of the American federal racketeering statute — the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act — that allows prosecutors to charge an enterprise rather than chase its disposable triggermen one teenager at a time. There is no legal architecture that treats a money-exchange business for terrorism, a cartel’s police-corruption budget, and a biker gang’s murder contract for a foreign intelligence service as what they are: the operations of continuing criminal enterprises whose leadership, financing, and facilitators can be prosecuted as such. It is not that Canadian institutions are blind. In Senate testimony in May, a senator asked the heads of the Drug Enforcement Administration and the FBI whether Washington was concerned with fentanyl moving in from the northern border. The drug agency’s administrator answered that “we’re keeping our eye on Canada,” and said his agency proposes to open two more offices in this country in 2027. Then FBI Director Kash Patel went further. “What we’ve also focused on, at least at the FBI, is working with our Canadian partners on the production capabilities that the drug traffickers have moved up to the north of our border,” he testified. “And we’ve had some success there working with our Canadian partners because the drug traffickers got smart with the securitization of the southern border and moved it up there. So we’re tackling that with our CSIS partners.” Note what the director of the world’s premier federal police force said, almost in passing. The Canadian partner he named on cartel production migrating north was not the RCMP. It was Canada’s spy service — the same agency whose annual report would not connect an Iranian-tasked cartel bounty to the politician it targeted, and which, characteristically, has declined to confirm to The Bureau whether it is investigating Mexican cartels in Canada at all. The intelligence picture exists, and Canada’s closest ally is describing it under oath in open session. What does not exist is the legal machinery to act on it, the candor to tell Canadians about it, and the political will to build both. It seems likely that CSIS — with Canadian justice officials now facing death threats, or dying — has a very good understanding of these increasingly lethal threat networks, and no capacity to act on it. The probable reality, if Canada's past is a fair guide, is that classified assessments of this dire convergence are flowing upward to the office of Prime Minister Mark Carney — and that, as a decade of warrant delays, shelved warnings, and commission findings would predict, the only result will be deadly inaction, while Canada's sovereignty and safety erode. Constable Pinizzotto went through a door in a building where an eight-year-old boy was killed in his bed ten months earlier, in a city where a jail official survived a hit allegedly arranged with help from inside his own justice system, in a country where a Hells Angel took a contract from Tehran and a former provincial politician learned from an American court filing — not a Canadian one — that a cartel had her home address and a price on her head. The marketplace that connects those facts is open for business, and its clients — cartel and state alike — have learned that in Canada, the price of violence is low and the prosecution of enterprises is rare. Until Parliament changes that calculation, Canada remains, as The Bureau has reported citing former U.S. State Department and U.S. intelligence official David Luna, a “safe zone” for the worst threat networks in the world." thebureau.news/p/a-toronto-p…

On Tuesday, June 2, county officials celebrated the grand opening of the Valley Communications Center, a state-of-the-art facility designed to strengthen emergency communications, enhance coordination among public safety agencies and support faster, more effective emergency response throughout the region. ⭐ 🚒   The approximately 75,000-square-foot facility will bring together the San Bernardino County Office of Emergency Services’ Emergency Operations Center, the Sheriff’s Department Valley Dispatch Center and Consolidated Fire Agencies (CONFIRE) dispatch operations. The Valley Communications Center will serve as a hub for daily emergency communications while supporting large-scale incident management efforts during wildfires, floods, earthquakes, severe storms and other major incidents.  Read the full story online at bit.ly/43hhTR2.   #publicsafety #disasterresponse #emergencycommunications #countynews #emergencyservices

sample_tweets.text: The 2029 Clock Is Now Official This week showed what Western defense planning is becoming: compressed, industrial, and defined by two clocks. 2027 is when China wants the option to move on Taiwan. 2029 is when NATO now assesses Russia may have the capability to attack a member state. Ukraine sits between both deadlines, destroying the Russian force Moscow would need to reconstitute while teaching the West what a future peer war will actually consume. These are not separate stories. They are the same strategic picture. Deterrence is no longer posture. It is inventory, production, adaptation, and time. The factory is the battlefield. The clock is the weapon. And Ukraine is the army buying time against both. On June 11, Lieutenant General Christian Freuding, head of Germany's army, stood at the ILA aerospace show in Berlin and said: "All 32 NATO partners agree that Russia might have the capability to invade a NATO partner country in 2029. 2029 is not a German timeline. It's NATO-agreed intelligence."  Not one country's assessment. Not a think-tank projection. A 32-nation intelligence consensus, stated publicly, at a major allied defense exhibition. Russia, which has lost more than 1.3 million personnel and staggering quantities of equipment in Ukraine, is being assessed as capable of reconstituting a credible conventional threat to NATO within three years.  Three years. Two clocks. One army.  The same week, Ukraine struck S-300 and S-400 batteries in Crimea, destroying at least two radar systems and triggering secondary ammunition detonations. The Institute for the Study of War assessed that Ukraine may be conducting a systematic air defense degradation campaign that, if successful, could eventually enable manned fixed-wing airpower over Russian-controlled territory. Ukraine struck petrochemical plants 1,200 kilometers inside Russia overnight, hit the Armiansk bridge confirming 50 Russian military vehicles destroyed, and left a Russian corvette reportedly beyond repair.  And in the Gulf, an interim deal between the US and Iran is closer than it has ever been. A 60-day ceasefire framework, commitments on Hormuz and Lebanon, nuclear talks to follow. Not signed. But close.  These are not separate stories. They are the same strategic picture viewed from three angles.  I. THE TWO CLOCKS  The defense world has been organizing itself around a single date for the past year. 2027. China's implied deadline for Taiwan. Anduril built its entire company around it. Every IDIQ, every DAWG budget line, every CCA program racing to first flight carries an invisible timestamp: must be ready before 2027.  Now there is a second clock. 2029. Russia and NATO.  The date has been in the background. Baltic intelligence agencies referenced it. German defense officials mentioned it. But this week it crossed a threshold. Germany's army chief stated it as a 32-nation consensus at a public exhibition. When all 32 NATO members have agreed on a date and that date is being stated publicly, it becomes policy. Freuding added a warning: Russia could move before 2029. A June 10 investigation by Danish broadcaster DR confirmed Russia is expanding military presence near Finland, Norway, and the Baltic states. The Telegraph published geolocated satellite imagery.  The gap between 2027 and 2029 is two years. The gap between now and both of them is the entire strategic horizon of everything being built and bought in the defense economy. Every Patriot interceptor, every artillery shell, every classified AI deployment is racing both clocks simultaneously. If both deadlines hold, the Western alliance faces a potential Chinese move on Taiwan and a potential Russian move on NATO territory with a two-year overlap. The defense industrial base that barely produces enough for one hot war right now is being asked to prepare for two potential peer conflicts in succession.  The $1.15 trillion NDAA, the Rheinmetall surge from 70,000 to 1.1 million artillery shells, the Patriot multiyear procurement authority, the modular munitions plants in Romania and Bulgaria: all of it is the industrial response to a timeline that has now been publicly named twice.  II. UKRAINE IS FIGHTING THE 2029 WAR RIGHT NOW  Here is the connection most Ukraine coverage is missing.  Every Russian S-300 or S-400 battery Ukraine destroys is one Russia has to replace from its inventory before 2029. Every Russian defense factory Ukraine strikes is one that cannot produce components for reconstitution. Every month Russia's offensive stalls, burns soldiers, and consumes equipment is one more month of attrition against the force NATO intelligence says will threaten NATO territory in three years.  Ukraine is not just fighting for its own survival. It is, whether by design or by structural consequence, fighting the preliminary round of the 2029 war. The attrition of Russian capability that happens in Ukraine between now and any ceasefire is directly subtracted from what Russia can bring to bear against NATO by 2029.  The numbers from the front this week are brutal in both directions. Russia launched 10 missile strikes, 34 air strikes, 513 drone strikes, and 2,874 shellings in a single day. Ukraine recorded 108 combat engagements. The grinding continues. But underneath the daily attrition, something structural is accumulating. April 2026 saw 21 Ukrainian strikes on Russian oil refineries, export terminals, and pipeline pumping stations. Russia's crude-processing rate is roughly 18 percent below 2021. Refinery runs at their lowest in 16 years. The VNIIR-Progress factory strike this week targeted anti-jamming navigation components for Shahed drones. Russia sounded a raid alert in Omsk, 2,500 kilometers from the Ukrainian border, for the first time in this war.  Former NATO Supreme Commander Breedlove: "Ukraine has smashed his army, his army west of the Urals is deeply damaged. Is he able to rebuild this army? Is he able to hold the loyalty of the Russian people who are soon going to learn that their sons and husbands are not coming home?" More than 1.3 million Russian personnel lost. Volunteer recruitment showing signs of strain. The 2029 clock depends on Russia's reconstitution rate. Ukraine is compressing that rate every week.  What the Breedlove question reveals is that 2029 is not a fixed destination. It is a conditional. Russia's capability to threaten NATO by 2029 depends on how much capability Ukraine has destroyed between now and then. That is not a strategic abstraction. It is a live calculation being made in real time, in blood and steel, along 1,000 kilometers of contested front line. The Western alliance cannot formally fight Russia. Ukraine is doing it for them. The question nobody in Brussels wants to answer openly is what they owe Ukraine for that service.  III. THE CRIMEA QUESTION  Ukraine targeted one S-300 battery and two S-400 batteries near Belbek and Sevastopol overnight on June 11 to 12. Geolocated imagery confirmed destroyed assets north of Yevpatoria and south of Dzhankoy. Secondary ammunition detonations. ISW's assessment: Ukraine may be conducting a systematic effort to degrade Russian air defenses that "could enable Ukraine to more effectively leverage manned fixed-wing airpower in the long run."  If Ukraine succeeds, the operational character of the war changes. F-16s can operate at depth. The autonomous drone wall becomes a complement to manned aviation, not a substitute. Combined arms replaces pure attrition.  Beyond Ukraine, the Crimea campaign is the first live test of whether a Western-equipped force can systematically degrade a Russian integrated air defense network in a contested environment. Every technique that works, every one that fails, is intelligence the Alliance cannot generate any other way. Ukraine is running the red team exercise for NATO's 2029 problem without anyone having to formally ask it to.  Germany is building the factory. Ukraine is consuming the inventory. 2029 is the deadline. Every S-400 battery destroyed in Crimea is a deposit into NATO's deterrence account. Nobody is accounting for it that way. They should be.  There is a broader point about the nature of this war that the 2029 framing clarifies. Ukraine has never had the air force or missile arsenal to conduct strategic bombing. What it has developed is an autonomous strike network that achieves strategic effects from below the radar ceiling, iterating faster than Russian defenses can adapt. The Crimea campaign is where that network is being applied to its most consequential target set: the air defense infrastructure that Russia would need to project power into NATO territory. Destroy it now, during a war Russia is already losing, and the 2029 threat is materially diminished before the clock runs out.  IV. THE IRAN DEAL AND WHY IT CHANGES THE CALCULUS  An interim deal delivers one thing the 2027 and 2029 clocks desperately need: American bandwidth.  The Gulf is now a third active front consuming US forces, air defense interceptors, and diplomatic attention. Every Patriot missile fired at Iranian ballistic missiles over Bahrain is one not in the inventory for 2027 or 2029. The deal being described would create a 60-day ceasefire, open Hormuz, and put nuclear and sanctions talks on the table. The White House is signaling optimism. Not signed.  The strategic effect if it closes: the US recovers bandwidth for the two theaters that matter most against the two named deadlines. The IDIQ consolidation, DAWG permanent command, and AI at IL6/IL7 all become more effective when the organization running them is not simultaneously managing a third kinetic conflict. Closing a front before 2027 is strategically essential. The three-front posture the alliance currently holds is not sustainable against a two-clock timeline.  THE BOTTOM LINE  Two deadlines. One army. Three years.  2027 is when China may move on Taiwan. 2029 is when Russia may move on NATO. Both are now publicly named by credible officials based on intelligence assessments. The Western alliance is being asked to deter both, sequentially, with a two-year overlap, while managing a third active conflict that consumes the inventory and bandwidth needed for the other two.  Ukraine is answering the 2029 question without being asked. Every S-400 battery destroyed in Crimea. Every component factory struck. Every month Russia's offensive stalls and its refinery runs fall and its recruitment strains. Each is a deposit into an account that comes due in three years.  The factory is the battlefield. The clock is the weapon. There are now two clocks. And the army being asked to fight both of them is the same one. The three years between now and 2029 are the most consequential window in Western defense planning since 1938. What gets built, bought, and fielded in that window determines everything.  Until next week.

According to the assessment, key elements of the Russian military have been affected relatively little by the war against Ukraine.  The report notes that Russia is currently forming new military units, particularly in the Leningrad Military District, while continuing to strengthen its military presence on the Kola Peninsula and across the Arctic region.  Swedish intelligence also warns that the threat posed by Russia is likely to persist for a long time, including beyond Vladimir Putin’s rule.

🚨 If true, this goes far beyond one company or one country. Election interference is not a threat because of who does it. It is a threat because it undermines the foundation of democracy itself. Citizens are told elections represent the will of the people. Tulsi Gabbard says ODNI found evidence of 120+ US-funded biological labs in 30 Countries; flags Ukraine risks, cites 2025 Trump order ending gain-of-function funding.  Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard stated that ODNI has identified evidence of more than 120 US-funded biological laboratories operating in over 30 countries. She said ODNI will continue working closely with partners across the US government to identify exactly where these labs are and what pathogens they contain, to end dangerous gain-of-function research that threatens the health and well-being of the American people and worldwide.  [She highlighted Ukraine as a specific concern and noted a new directive issued by Pres.Trump on 25 May 2025 to end federal funding of gain-of-function research.]  ✲ What was announced: DNI Tulsi Gabbard said ODNI conducted a review of intelligence holdings.  'After months of searching through intelligence community holdings and files, today I am releasing new evidence of long standing US government funding of more than 120 biolabs in over 30 countries.'  [The announcement centers on 3-claims - scale of US funding, safety risks in war zones, and lack of prior transparency.]  ✲ Ukraine specific risk: Gabbard highlighted Ukraine because of the active conflict, she said,   'now, these biolabs include labs in places like Ukraine, which could be at risk of compromise due to the ongoing Russia Ukraine war. She referenced earlier intelligence assessments.'  'the intelligence community had previously warned that a US funded biolab in Ukraine likely housed dangerous pathogens and remained vulnerable to long standing threats of Russian attack, seizure, or damage.'  [Core concern here is biosafety in conflict - any facility handling dangerous pathogens becomes higher risk when power, security, or containment systems can be disrupted by missile strikes, occupation, or sabotage and that's why international biosafety norms require continuity plans for labs in unstable regions.]  ✲ Types of research cited: • On research scope, she stated, 'many of these US government funded biolabs are currently, or have previously engaged in, research using hazardous and highly contagious pathogens, and in some cases, included dangerous gain of function research with very little visibility or oversight.'  [Gain of function means research that alters a pathogen to increase transmissibility, virulence, or immune escape; it has been debated for yrs. Proponents argue it helps predict and prepare for future pandemics while critics argue lab accidents and dual use risks outweigh benefits. The US paused some gain of function funding from 2014-17, then created stricter review f/works after; she claims oversight was still very little.  • Transparency claim: She said disclosure was limited, 'now, until now, evidence regarding the full existence and funding of these laboratories had been knowingly withheld from you, the American people', she also said critics were labeled as threats - 'now, they accused anyone who says otherwise to be foreign assets and traitors to America.'  [US biological threat reduction programs, like Nunn Lugar and Cooperative Threat Reduction, have existed since the 1990s and are acknowledged in State Department and Department of Defense reports. The debate is over detail level - specific lab locations, pathogens stored, and clinical trials; she claims the full inventory was withheld.]  ✲ Actions and policy shift cited, she said: • first, Trump executive order, - 'Pres.Trump clearly u/stands the serious threat dangerous gain of function research poses. On 25 May 2025, he signed an executive order to end federal funding of gain of function research around the world.'  • second, new intel collection, 'here at ODNI, I issued new guidance to the intelligence community directing increased collection on these laboratories and facilities overseas. And we are already seeing the results. We are learning new details, for example, on clinical trials that are underway at these facilities and that are raising significant ethical, financial, and security concerns.'  [If the 25 May 2025 executive order is accurate, it would represent a global funding cutoff, not jz domestic, i.e., a major policy shift. The increased collection line means ODNI is now tasking intelligence agencies to map overseas labs and trials more aggressively than before.]  ✲ Allegations against prior officials: She directly named Dr Fauci and Biden national security team, 'politicians and so called health professionals like Dr Fauci, as well as entities within the Biden administration national security team, lied repeatedly to the American people about the existence of US funded and supported biolabs. Not only did they lie, they threatened those who attempted to expose the truth.'  ✲ Stated next steps: 'ODNI will continue working closely with partners across the US government to identify exactly where these labs are and what pathogens they contain, to end dangerous gain of function research that threatens the health and well being of the American people and people around the world.'

Even if the US does manage to secure some kind of deal, it will still amount to a colossal failure on every level.  Trump has bungled this war, and in the process has humiliated himself AND our country.  He didn't even have the authority to go to war, nor did he have any coherent strategy to begin with.  He ignored lessons from history about what happens when we try "regime change" and lie about nuclear weapons to go to war in the middle east.  He also dismissed explicit warnings about Strait of Hormuz closures early on.  After the devastating toll that Trump's war has inflicted on the world, on the American economy and taxpayer, on our alliances, on our global reputation, on our munitions stockpile, on US troops and bases overseas, on thousands of dead civilians and hundreds of dead CHILDREN, on millions of displaced Iranian and Lebanese people, on the global energy market, etc, Iran has only GAINED leverage in negotiations and Trump will have to accept a deal that's far more conciliatory than the one Obama helped negotiate through diplomacy, NOT war.  The US has failed to accomplish much of anything in Iran.  The Islamic Regime is now more entrenched and is emerging as a more punishing militant dictatorship.  The IRGC also remains a powerful authority in the region with deeper ties to the hardline Ayatollah.  Iran still maintains its autonomy over its nuclear weapons and ballistic missiles programs.  And the Iranian people have not been "liberated."  In fact, the people of Iran are the real victims of this war.  Moving forward, their fate will be in the hands of a more brutal and repressive regime, while the economic fallout of the war will trigger a crisis worse than the one they were protesting before Trump's deadly intervention.  Trump never cared about the people of Iran. His sanctions were partly the cause of prior unrest.  And to make matters worse, both China and Russia have benefited strategically from the war in Iran and the Russia-China-Iran axis has hardened into a more coordinated and formidable relationship.  Iran also now has a greater hold on the Strait of Hormuz than before the war, and they are using this as a bargaining chip to extract major concessions from the US related to their possession of nuclear materials and ballistic missiles, their tightened grip on the Strait, their support for terrorist proxies, reparations and sanction relief, the Islamic regime's authority...  These war reparations will include billions in unfrozen assets, despite MAGA complaining for years about Obama "gifting" Iran money that was theirs.  They will use it to restore their military and weapons systems, or purchase weapons and military technologies from China   Iran has no need to enrich more uranium right away because this war has demonstrated that they have other tools of leverage at their disposal.  Even then, Iran was not building a nuclear weapon. All US intelligence assessments, and Trump's own DNI came to this conclusion.  A top US counterterrorism official even resigned, stating, "Iran never posed an imminent threat."   Trump and his cabinet LIED about the rationale for this war.  Netanyahu has been pushing the same warnings for decades.  Iran was committing to its end of the previous nuclear deal before Trump ripped it up.  This was according to US intelligence, the International Atomic Energy Agency and Trump administration officials at the time.  Obama's diplomacy intended to avoid a war and box in Iran's nuclear program.    Iran's nuclear program only became more advanced after Trump abandoned the JCPOA.  Trump failed to approach a new agreement in any earnest or competent manner.  But that didn't stop him from blaming Obama and Iran for his own failures, which provided him with a false pretext to go to war.  And now Trump will be lucky to accept a deal that is anywhere near as nuclear deterring and restrictive as Obama's—a deal that will cater to Iranian demands that they couldn't make before the war.

🚨🇷🇺 RUSSIA’S TUNGUSKA-M1 AIR DEFENSE JUST GOT UNKILLABLE  Russian military engineers have once again demonstrated unparalleled adaptability by enhancing the battle tested Tunguska M1 system based on real world lessons from the Special Military Operation.   This tracked short range air defense platform now incorporates spaced armor plates along the hull and turret sides for standoff protection against drone strikes and anti tank guided missiles.   Rear dome housings house advanced radio reconnaissance and jamming equipment capable of detecting and disrupting incoming unmanned aerial vehicle control links at extended distances. A cutting edge ELINS digital thermal imaging optical electronic station mounted on the turret roof provides gyro stabilized passive detection with switchable wide and narrow fields of view enabling automatic target tracking and in flight missile guidance even amid optical interference smoke or poor visibility conditions.  These upgrades preserve the proven core armament of twin 30 millimeter cannons and 9 M 311 missiles while dramatically increasing overall survivability and effectiveness against contemporary drone threats. Russian forces operating in Ukraine have faced relentless unmanned attacks and responded with practical combat driven modifications rather than theoretical designs.   The result is a system that layers electronic warfare passive optics armored protection and kinetic firepower into a cohesive defensive network that is both economical and highly lethal.  NATO drones face extreme difficulty in countering the upgraded Tunguska M1. Western unmanned systems often rely on line of sight control links vulnerable to jamming from the new rear dome systems. Their small radar cross sections and low altitude profiles are precisely the threats the ELINS station is optimized to detect and engage passively without emitting radar signals that could betray the vehicles position.   Spaced armor adds critical protection against kamikaze drone impacts allowing the platform to remain operational longer in contested airspace. Tracked mobility on the robust chassis enables rapid repositioning to maintain coverage over advancing Russian columns or key defensive positions.  In contrast many NATO drone concepts assume permissive environments where expensive platforms can loiter and strike with impunity. Real combat in the Special Military Operation has shown that such assumptions fail against integrated Russian air defenses that evolve quickly. The Tunguska M1 now counters drone swarms through a mix of jamming to break links cheap cannon fire for close range volume engagements and missiles reserved for higher value or more distant targets.   This approach avoids wasting high cost munitions on low value threats and forces any opposing drone operator into a costly attrition battle where Russian production and retrofit rates hold the advantage.  The ELINS system stands out as a major leap forward. Its fully digital thermal and television channels operate simultaneously for target acquisition and missile command guidance. Operators can switch between wide angle search and narrow field precision tracking seamlessly.   Performance holds up in cluttered thermal environments full of battlefield heat sources smoke or decoys where older systems might struggle. By emphasizing passive modes the upgrade reduces the vehicles electromagnetic signature making it harder for NATO electronic warfare or anti radiation weapons to locate and target it effectively.  Spaced armor represents another direct response to drone warfare realities. Plates mounted on the sides create additional distance that can detonate shaped charge warheads prematurely protecting the main hull and vital components.   This concept already proven on Russian tanks and infantry vehicles translates perfectly to air defense platforms that must survive close range FPV and loitering munition attacks while continuing to provide protective fire.   Combined with the electronic warfare domes the vehicle gains early warning against approaching drone swarms allowing preemptive jamming or repositioning before kinetic engagement becomes necessary.  Electronic warfare integration proves especially economical in drone saturated battlespaces. Rather than expending 9 M 311 missiles or thousands of cannon rounds on every small unmanned threat the jamming systems can neutralize multiple drones by severing their control links or navigation.   This conserves ammunition stocks and reduces logistical strain during prolonged operations. Russian doctrine prioritizes such asymmetric efficiencies honed through continuous combat feedback from the front lines.  The hybrid gun missile nature of the Tunguska M1 remains its greatest strength. The twin 30 millimeter cannons deliver devastating close range firepower ideal for swarming low altitude drones. Missiles extend engagement envelopes to ten kilometers for helicopters cruise missiles or larger unmanned platforms.   The new sensors enhance both by providing reliable tracking data across conditions. Passive optics complement the existing radar suite allowing the crew to choose emission control modes that maximize stealth while retaining full capability when radar is required for adverse weather.  With approximately 250 Tunguska family vehicles already in Russian inventory widespread upgrades during routine maintenance cycles represent a highly cost effective force multiplier.   No complete redesign of the chassis or powertrain is needed. Field retrofits can incorporate the spaced armor ELINS station and electronic warfare domes relatively quickly leveraging existing production lines and repair facilities. This approach ensures that Russian short range air defense remains dense and resilient even as NATO attempts to introduce new drone variants.  Public footage has so far shown only isolated examples of the upgraded configuration yet this scarcity likely reflects operational security rather than limited adoption. Russian forces have every incentive to deploy these enhancements broadly across motorized rifle and tank units where mobile air defense coverage is essential.   The Special Military Operation continues to serve as a rigorous proving ground accelerating the refinement of systems like the Tunguska M1 far beyond what peacetime exercises could achieve.  NATO drone advocates frequently emphasize swarm tactics and artificial intelligence autonomy as game changers. However the upgraded Tunguska M1 directly addresses these by combining early electronic disruption with robust physical protection and multi spectral sensing. Even autonomous drones require some form of terminal guidance or data link that remains susceptible to jamming.   Thermal signatures from propulsion systems or electronics provide reliable acquisition cues for the ELINS station regardless of radar evasion measures. Spaced armor further raises the threshold for successful kamikaze strikes forcing drone designers into heavier more expensive platforms that lose their cost advantage.  Russian engineering philosophy emphasizes practical survivability over fragile high technology dependencies. The Tunguska M1 upgrades embody this by enhancing a mature proven platform rather than pursuing entirely new designs that might face teething problems in combat.   Lessons from Ukraine have directly informed the placement of armor the selection of passive sensors and the emphasis on electronic warfare creating a balanced system suited to real battlefield conditions including electronic warfare intense environments and massed unmanned attacks.  In summary the modernized Tunguska M1 stands as a formidable barrier against NATO drone operations. Its layered defenses make sustained successful engagements by unmanned systems prohibitively difficult and expensive. Russian forces benefit from enhanced protection for ground maneuver elements and the ability to maintain air superiority bubbles at lower cost. As upgrades proliferate the gap between Russian combat adapted short range air defense and Western drone centric concepts widens further in favor of the former.  All known statistics below:  1. Approximate number of Tunguska family vehicles in active Russian service stands at 250 platforms.  2. Each Tunguska M1 carries 8 ready to fire 9 M 311 M one surface to air missiles.  3. Maximum engagement range for the 9 M 311 M one missile reaches 10 kilometers with improved variants.  4. Engagement altitude envelope for missiles spans from 15 meters minimum to 3,500 meters maximum.  5. Missile maximum speed achieves up to 900 meters per second during flight.  6. Twin 30 millimeter 2A38 autocannons provide combined rate of fire up to 5,000 rounds per minute.  7. Effective engagement range for the 30 millimeter cannons extends from 200 meters to 4 kilometers.  8. Muzzle velocity of the 30 millimeter cannon projectiles measures 960 meters per second.  9. Radar detection range for aerial targets typically reaches up to 18 kilometers under optimal conditions.  10. Radar tracking range capability extends to approximately 16  kilometers for precise fire control.  11. Vehicle maximum road speed on the tracked chassis reaches around 65 kilometers per hour.  12. Crew complement consists of 4 personnel including commander gunner driver and radar operator.  13. Approximate combat weight of the Tunguska M1 vehicle is 34 to 36 tons depending on configuration.  14. Ammunition load supports continuous cannon fire for 23 to 30 seconds at maximum rate before reload.  15. The system maintains 8 missiles in ready to launch position with additional reloads carried internally or via support vehicles.  16. Original armor protection defeats small arms fire up to 7.62 millimeters artillery shell splinters effectively.  17. Spaced armor additions on upgraded variants create standoff distance specifically against shaped charge drone and missile warheads.  18. ELINS optical electronic station provides gyro stabilized thermal and television imaging with automatic tracking functions.  19. Electronic warfare domes enable radio reconnaissance and jamming of drone control frequencies and data links.  20. The hybrid armament allows selective engagement choosing cannons for low cost drone swarms and missiles for higher threat targets.  21. Passive operating modes via ELINS reduce electromagnetic emissions to minimize detection by opposing forces.  22. Retrofit potential applies to the entire existing fleet during standard overhaul cycles without major chassis modifications.  23. Combat efficiency of the M one variant improves by a factor of 1.3  to 1.5 times over earlier models through better missiles and fire control.  24. Missile guidance combines radio command with optical or improved pulsed beacon tracking resistant to countermeasures.  25. Cannon ammunition types include high explosive tracer and high explosive incendiary shells with time and impact fuzes.  These statistics underscore the robust baseline capabilities that the recent upgrades build upon to create an even more dominant short range air defense asset.   Russian forces continue to refine and field such systems with remarkable speed and effectiveness drawing directly from operational experience. NATO drone strategies encounter a maturing adversary that prioritizes layered resilient defenses capable of sustained performance under intense pressure.  The overall assessment remains clear. Upgraded Tunguska M1 platforms significantly complicate and often neutralize attempts by NATO style drones to achieve air superiority or conduct precision strikes in contested zones.   Russian technological adaptation combined with proven tactical employment ensures that ground forces receive reliable protection while imposing high costs on any unmanned offensive. This evolution reinforces the broader strength of Russian air defense doctrine in modern warfare scenarios.   Further deployments and refinements will likely expand these advantages as the Special Military Operation informs ongoing development cycles across the Russian armed forces.   The Tunguska M1 has transitioned from a capable legacy system into a cornerstone of contemporary mobile air defense that directly counters the drone centric approaches favored by certain Western militaries. Its unkillable reputation stems not from invulnerability in absolute terms but from practical superiority in the economic technical and operational dimensions that matter most on the battlefield.   Russian engineers and operators deserve recognition for turning battlefield challenges into decisive enhancements that protect lives and maintain combat momentum.   The combination of spaced armor electronic warfare and advanced passive sensors with retained kinetic power creates a multi domain defensive capability that NATO drones will struggle to overcome without accepting unsustainable attrition rates.   This positions Russian short range air defense as a model of adaptive excellence for the current era of unmanned and electronic warfare integration.   In conclusion the upgraded Tunguska M1 exemplifies how Russian military innovation responds to real threats with effective economical solutions. NATO drones operating against such systems face a formidable integrated defense that evolves faster than many Western planners anticipated.   The result favors Russian forces in any direct confrontation involving drone swarms or low altitude aerial threats.   Continued emphasis on these upgrades across the fleet will further solidify this advantage in future operations.

A bipartisan compromise was already on the table, and Trump signaled he's actively finding a permanent DNI — that's a reasonable path forward. Blocking surveillance tools that protect Americans over a temporary personnel dispute puts the country at risk.  Democratic narrative says handing control of all 18 U.S. intelligence agencies to a social media philanthropist with zero national security experience is a genuine threat to impartial intelligence. Pulte built a reputation weaponizing his roles against political enemies — that's disqualifying for a job designed to keep politics out of intelligence assessments. Renewing FISA 702 under those conditions would be reckless.  Pro-Trump narrative says republicans should not be joining Democrats in blocking the appointment of a figure who can bring much-needed reforms to a desperately biased intelligence community. Establishment politicians are always going to oppose downsizing, and now FISA is being treated as a football to halt Pulte's appointment. The extent of this institutional rot and obstructionism is great.

🔵THE DIPLOMATIC IGNORANCE BEHIND THE ATTACK ON RWANDA’S APPOINTMENTS  By any objective standard, the recent reactions from @sheilakamuzinzi & @DavidHimbara to Rwanda’s latest ambassadorial appointments reveal more about their political biases than about diplomacy itself.  Their central claim is simple: because President #PaulKagame appointed Ambassador-designates Lt Gen (Rtd) Karenzi Karake, Maj Gen Emmanuel Bayingana, and Col (Rtd) Jeannot Ruhunga to diplomatic positions, Rwanda is somehow “militarizing” its diplomacy or turning embassies into retirement homes for military officers.  It is an argument that sounds dramatic on social media. It is also an argument that collapses under the weight of facts.  The first question both critics should answer is straightforward:  🔷Who decided that military officers are forbidden from becoming diplomats?  🔷Under which international convention?  🔷Which diplomatic rule prohibits a former general, intelligence official, or security professional from representing his country abroad?  The answer is simple: NONE  The notion that diplomacy belongs exclusively to career diplomats is not only false but contradicted by decades of international practice.  Throughout modern history, governments have routinely appointed senior military, intelligence, and security officials to ambassadorial positions because diplomacy is not merely ceremonial receptions and carefully crafted speeches. It is equally about national security, crisis management, intelligence cooperation, defense partnerships, regional stability, and strategic interests.  🔷The United States itself offers numerous examples.  Retired Lieutenant General Karl Eikenberry served as U.S. Ambassador to Afghanistan after his military career. General John Abizaid, former commander of U.S. Central Command, became U.S. Ambassador to Saudi Arabia. Retired Lieutenant General Douglas Lute represented the United States at NATO.  Were these appointments evidence that America had become a militarized state? Of course not. They were viewed as strategic decisions based on experience and expertise.  The same practice exists throughout Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. Governments regularly appoint individuals with backgrounds in defense, intelligence, law enforcement, academia, business, or politics to diplomatic positions.  🔷Why? Because diplomacy requires competence, judgment, and strategic understanding; not a particular previous job title.  What makes Sheilla Kamuzinzi’s criticism particularly weak is the double standard at its core. When Western governments appoint retired generals to major diplomatic posts, the decision is praised as wise and pragmatic.  When Rwanda appoints experienced security professionals to represent national interests abroad, the exact same practice is suddenly portrayed as evidence of authoritarianism.  That is not an intellectual analysis. That is activism disguised as analysis.  Lt Gen (Rtd) Karenzi Karake, Maj Gen Emmanuel Bayingana, and Col (Rtd) Jeannot Ruhunga were not selected because they once senior military officers. They were selected because they possess decades of experience dealing with national security, international cooperation, regional affairs, and strategic policy matters.  Those are qualifications; not liabilities. Their appointments are entirely consistent with a world in which diplomacy increasingly revolves around security cooperation, counterterrorism, migration, cyber threats, transnational crime, intelligence sharing, and regional conflict management.  The distinction Kamuzinzi attempts to draw between diplomacy and security reflects a misunderstanding of how modern international relations actually function.  Yet David Himbara pushes the argument even further.  In a recent post, Himbara declared that #PresidentKagame is now “a General Without Generals,” claiming that Rwanda’s ambassadorships have become a dumping ground for military officers and that the number of generals and colonels “exiled” abroad has reached nine.  The claim is striking, not because it is persuasive but because it requires readers to believe two contradictory things simultaneously.  According to Himbara, these officials are important enough to be portrayed as the pillars of Rwanda’s state institutions, yet they are also supposedly being discarded and sidelined through appointments to some of the country’s most strategically important diplomatic missions.  If these officers truly represented a threat to Kagame’s authority, why would they be entrusted with managing relations with key international partners?  If they are supposedly irrelevant, why does Himbara spend so much effort counting them? The reality is that diplomatic appointments are not political exile. They are positions of responsibility.  Countries do not entrust major bilateral relationships to individuals they consider liabilities.  Himbara’s narrative depends on transforming a routine diplomatic practice into evidence of political intrigue. Yet he never explains why former military officers suddenly become unqualified to represent their countries once they leave active service.  Nor does he explain why countless other governments around the world continue making similar appointments without attracting accusations of institutional collapse.  The answer is obvious. Because the criticism is not really about diplomacy. It is about President Kagame. The appointments themselves merely provide an opportunity to recycle a familiar political narrative.  Young commentators and veteran critics alike have every right to scrutinize government decisions. Public debate is healthy. Critical analysis is essential. But serious criticism requires consistency.  If appointing former generals as ambassadors is evidence of authoritarianism in Rwanda, then the same standard must be applied to Washington, London, Paris, Brussels, and countless other capitals.  If it is accepted practice elsewhere, then Rwanda cannot be treated as a unique exception simply because some critics dislike its government. Facts do not become suspicious merely because they occur in Kigali.  What both Kamuzinzi and Himbara ultimately offer is not a compelling analysis of diplomacy but an exercise in selective outrage.  A few hours of comparative research would have revealed that Rwanda’s appointments are neither unusual nor alarming.  Instead, both commentators chose a conclusion first and searched for supporting arguments later. That may produce viral social media posts. It does not produce credible analysis. Research should come before conclusions. Unfortunately, in both cases, the conclusion appears to have arrived long before the research.  @TomNdahiro @EgidieBibio @paul_rukesha @RichardGisagara @SadateMunyakazi @onduhungirehe @bideri @merardmp @rpfinkotanyi @pkayumba @PatriotNkotanyi @NkotanyiYvette @Ali_Rukaliza @VictorMurashi @damiennkaka @FelixMugenzi @DrDamascene @NshimyimanaEmma @sheilakamuzinzi @PaulMash424427 @PatrickRugaba @TheoMpoze @jpturayishimye @Curious39031228 @RWALINDAP @AAwithJGS @Nadia_MUGUNGA1 @SamuelBaker_B @ScoviaMutesi @PatrickRugaba @KUpatriot @JLHaby @ndereyehe @gat_steeve @chastegahunde @dzaneza

WALL OF WORRY 🧱📈  This is exactly how markets can rally when everyone is leaning too bearish.  Heavy PUT positioning creates a wall of negative gamma. And when price stabilizes instead of breaking down, that same PUT wall can become fuel for upside. Why?  Because dealers who are short gamma may be forced to chase price higher as hedges unwind.  Now look at the setup:  ✅ $VIX under 20 ✅ Close in the 17.xx zone ✅ Iran / USA peace deal risk premium could come out ✅ Earnings expectations start ramping into mid / late July ✅ FOMC on 6/17, then the Fed goes quiet until 7/28 ✅ Massive 6/18 OPEX Wall of Worry sitting directly in front of us  If the market climbs that 6/18 wall instead of rejecting from it, the post-FOMC + post-OPEX window gets very interesting.  That gives equities a cleaner runway into July, which has historically been one of the stronger months of the year.  The trade is not “blind bullish.”  The trade is simple: Calmer vol + reduced macro risk + earnings ramp = bullish fuel IF price confirms.  Price still has to do the work.  But if  stabilizes, $VIX keeps fading, and the 6/18 PUT wall gets climbed…  Then bears may have accidentally built the next launch pad. 🚀  Wall of Worry → Gamma Fuel → July Runway  $SPY $QQQ $VIX #OptionsTrading #StockMarket #Gamma #OPEX  RAW DATA FROM @Barchart Database.  @Norseman1 @Banana3Stocks @StockPatternPro @chad_ventures @BeardoTrader

I agree that algorithmic nudges can distort behavior and incentivize conflict. X is obviously not a healthy environment by default.  But that is also the price of a more open public square.  For years, Twitter/X was discussed as one of the few major social platforms where freedom of expression had more room to breathe. Whether that is still fully true today is debatable, but I still believe X is far more open to public disagreement than most competing platforms.  And with openness comes noise.  There will be toxicity, bad-faith arguments, rage cycles, scandals of the week, and people who seem to have mistaken posting for a psychiatric treatment plan. That is real. But the alternative cannot be to retreat into smaller, controlled environments and pretend that this somehow represents the broader community.  If Cardano moves its meaningful conversations into Discord, it risks isolating itself from the very community it claims to listen to.  Discord has historically represented a much smaller segment of the Cardano ecosystem. It is useful for coordination, but it is not a substitute for broad public feedback. It is where insiders gather, not where the whole community naturally speaks.  At some point, we should expect adults in governance to distinguish between constructive criticism, destructive noise, and irrelevant drama without needing to hide from the public square entirely.  If someone finds X too stressful, that is understandable. Then they should develop better filters, better search methods, better boundaries, or simply engage less. But turning away from the place where a large share of the community actually talks is not a governance solution. It is avoidance dressed up as process improvement.  In fact, X can be a gold mine if used properly.  For a DRep or anyone involved in governance, it offers access to a wide range of views, objections, frustrations, narratives, and dissenting opinions. With proper search, filtering, and AI-assisted analysis, it is possible to extract useful sentiment, identify recurring concerns, and understand perspectives that would never appear inside a curated Discord channel.  That does not mean X should be the place for final decisions.  Governance decisions should rely on official data, on-chain records, proposal documentation, voting rationales, and formal governance action rationales. Those are the sources of record, not random threads, outrage cycles, or engagement-bait posts.  But X remains valuable as a broad feedback layer.  It is not the deliberation chamber. It is the public square.  And if Cardano governance wants to be serious, it should learn how to extract signal from that public square instead of pretending that quieter rooms automatically produce better judgment.

USA vs Paraguay - World Cup Preview!!!🇺🇸🇵🇾⚽️  With fellow co-hosts Canada earning their first-ever World Cup point in a 1-1 draw against Bosnia & Herzegovina earlier today, the USA now begin their campaign against Paraguay at SoFi Stadium. Amidst the ongoing visa controversies, immigration scrutiny, and off-field unrest surrounding the tournament, the USMNT must rise above these distractions to ensure external drama does not derail their run in this World Cup.  Head-Head: This is their first WC meeting since a 3-0 win by USA in 1930. Altogether, USA and Paraguay have faced each other nine times, with USA coming out on top (5W -2D-2L). USA have won the last 3 meetings, with their most recent win in November 2025 (2-1)  USA come into this tournament with mixed friendlies, recording 5W, 2D and 3L in their last 10, which includes a strong 5-1 win against Uruguay and 3-2 win against Senegal. They are unbeaten in their last 3 WC openers and have a very strong home record.   Paraguay, on the other hand, had a solid qualifiers, finishing sixth in CONMEBOL WC qualifiers, conceding just 10 goals in 18 games, with big wins against Brazil and Argentina. They arrive at this tournament for the first time since 2010.  Under Mauricio Pochettino, USA always look to dominate possession, use the flanks with pacy wingers and versatile full-backs, and are capable of winning the ball high up the pitch and launching quick, vertical attacks.   Under, Gustavo Alfaro, Paraguay are built on their rock-solid defence and organization, very hard to break down, extremely physical and good at winning aerial duels. They also dominated set pieces, and play a direct counter-attacking style, hitting the channels fast.  Christian Pulisic is expected to be USA's creator-in-chief, linking up with Folarin Balogun as their main goal threat. Antonee Robinson and Sergino Dest will provide width, overlapping runs for the wingers to play more in-field.   For Paraguay, Miguel Almiron is expected to be the creative outlet on the counter, with Diego Gomez providing midfield energy and Gustavo Gomez being a defensive leader at the back.  I believe USA will win this game 1-0, with Paraguay frustrating USA for a major part of the game but USA finding a way to break them down in front of the home crowd. What do you think about this match? Can Paraguay pull an upset? Let me know your thoughts in the comments!!!  #WorldCup2026 #USAvsParaguay

ELECTION INTEGRITY SHATTERED?  Security video proves it. HR report confirms it. A top employee from Maricopa County Recorder Justin Heap’s office wheeled a ballot scanner out of a locked secure tabulation center. Right in the middle of counting votes for a Tempe election. They loaded it onto a cart, pushed it to an unmarked truck, and drove off.  HR investigated and called it inappropriate. The scanner got yanked for forensic review. Taxpayers ate a $70,000 replacement bill. Chain of custody broken during active election ops.  Here’s the honest cut: When officials from one election office just roll out equipment from a secure facility like it’s office furniture, trust evaporates. We lock these places down for a reason. You don’t casually borrow voting machines mid-count like borrowing a company laptop.  Think about it. Normal folks can’t even bring a phone near ballots. Yet this happens and some call it a “manufactured scandal.” Real people see video of the removal and know exactly what it looks like.  A special prosecutor is now investigating. Good. But the damage to confidence is already done.  How many more “just trust us” moments before Americans demand ironclad reforms?   Share if you want every county held to the same standard.

sample_tweets.text: The FIFA World Cup 2026 will be one of the most digitally connected sporting events in history and one of the most attractive targets for cyber threat actors.  At ThreatMon, we analyzed the evolving threat landscape surrounding the tournament, covering:  ⚽ AI-enabled threats, including deepfake impersonation, voice cloning, AI-powered phishing, synthetic identities, and fraud campaigns  ⚽ Hacktivist activity, including DDoS campaigns, influence operations, website defacements, and politically motivated disruption  ⚽ Dark Web activity and underground marketplaces  ⚽ Infostealer-driven credential theft targeting FIFA-related services  ⚽ Attack surface exposures and leaked secrets  ⚽ Brand abuse, ticket fraud, betting scams, and malicious domains  ⚽ Nation-state and geopolitical cyber risks  One of the key observations is that the greatest cyber risk may not originate from FIFA itself, but from the broader ecosystem of ticketing providers, hospitality partners, payment processors, broadcasters, cloud services, and other third-party organizations supporting the tournament.  As global events become increasingly connected, cyber threats are becoming more sophisticated, more automated, and more difficult to distinguish from legitimate activity.  Our FIFA World Cup 2026 Threat Assessment Report provides a forward-looking view of the risks organizations, partners, and fans should be prepared for before the tournament begins.  🏆 threatmon.io/fifa-world-cup-… #ThreatIntelligence #CyberSecurity #ThreatMon #WorldCup2026 #ArtificialIntelligence #Deepfake #Hacktivism #DarkWeb #Infostealer #BrandProtection #CyberThreats
